role_summary: Southern Christian Leadership Conference Director of Direct Action (1962-1968); trained by James Lawson at the Nashville workshops; strategist of the May 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade (the decision to send school-age children into the streets, which dramatically intensified the campaign's media impact); director of the 1965 Selma voting rights campaign; co-initiator (with Martin Luther King Jr.) of the 1968 Poor People's Campaign.
ai_reading: Bevel is named across the oral histories far more often than the catalog's two standing references to him would suggest (the SNCC-Nashville-organizers cohort and the Freeman Hrabowski (entry 45) bio, where his strategy as the architect of the 1963 Birmingham Children's Crusade is the structural condition that put Hrabowski in jail at age twelve). At least five interviewees name him by name in their testimony: Charles McLaurin (entry 17), Lawrence Guyot (entry 76), C. T. Vivian (entry 134), Wyatt Tee Walker (entry 132), and Jamila Jones (entry 58). He is not himself a Civil Rights History Project interviewee. His standalone catalog page anchors the Lawson Nashville-workshop alumni cohort alongside LaFayette, Lewis, Nash, Barry, Vivian, and others; it also surfaces the catalog's clearest documented case of a strategic-decision-shaping-an-interviewee's-life-arc, the Children's Crusade design choice that produced the Hrabowski biographical anchor. The novel observation his page surfaces is the strategy-credit distribution problem: Bevel originated the Children's Crusade tactic; King decided to deploy it; the historiography credits both in roughly equal measure; the embedding's references to the May 1963 Birmingham campaign in the corpus's catalog pages run primarily through King and the campaign-as-a-whole rather than through Bevel by name.
biographical_paragraph: Bevel was born October 19, 1936 in Itta Bena, Mississippi. He enlisted in the U.S. Navy as a teenager, served four years, and on discharge enrolled at American Baptist Theological Seminary in Nashville in 1959, where he joined James Lawson's Tuesday-night nonviolence workshops alongside Bernard LaFayette, John Lewis, Diane Nash, and others. He participated in the 1961 Freedom Rides and married Diane Nash in 1961. In 1962 he joined the Southern Christian Leadership Conference as Director of Direct Action, the strategist role he would hold through 1968. In May 1963 he proposed the tactical decision that became the Birmingham Children's Crusade: sending school-age Black children into the streets in waves so that the campaign could continue past the point where adult arrests had exhausted the Birmingham movement. The strategy's success (the Connor fire-hose and police-dog response, then-Vice-President Lyndon Johnson's pressure on John F. Kennedy to introduce civil rights legislation) is documented in Adam Fairclough's To Redeem the Soul of America (University of Georgia Press, 1987), the canonical scholarly history of SCLC. He directed the 1965 Selma voting rights campaign and co-initiated the 1968 Poor People's Campaign with King. After King's 1968 assassination Bevel's organizational positions became increasingly heterodox; he ran briefly for the U.S. Presidency in 1992 on Lyndon LaRouche's ticket and later served as Louis Farrakhan's Nation of Islam minister in Chicago. He died December 19, 2008 in Springfield, Virginia at seventy-two.

interview_snippets.speaker: C. T. Vivian
interview_snippets.lead_in: Asked about the internal SCLC debate over sending school-age children into the Birmingham streets in May 1963, C. T. Vivian credits Bevel with the decision:
interview_snippets.quote: Bevel was the one that really said young people should go, right? That you're never too young to fight for your freedom became the thing for all of us, but I mean it was Bevel who brought it up and really fought for it, and Diane of course, they were married at the time.

interview_snippets.speaker: Wyatt Tee Walker
interview_snippets.lead_in: Wyatt Tee Walker, SCLC executive director during the Birmingham campaign, recalls the choice to use children in the demonstrations:
interview_snippets.quote: And James Bevel and we decided we'd use the children.

interview_snippets.speaker: Charles McLaurin
interview_snippets.lead_in: Charles McLaurin describes the night Bevel walked into a Jackson, Mississippi pool hall in the Georgetown neighborhood, where teenagers were arming for a fight, and interrupted them:
interview_snippets.quote: And he hopped up on a pool table and said, hold it. He said, you all you're brave. You're bad. You're going to go out there and kill one another.

interview_snippets.speaker: Lawrence Guyot
interview_snippets.lead_in: Lawrence Guyot places Bevel in the room the night Fannie Lou Hamer first volunteered to register to vote:
interview_snippets.quote: James Bevel, myself, [Charles] McLaurin, and [James] Forman go to a mass meeting. We open the call for anyone who wants to go down and register. Fannie Lou Hamer raises her hand.

interview_snippets.speaker: Jamila Jones
interview_snippets.lead_in: Jamila Jones, a Montgomery freedom singer, recalls SCLC dispatching Bevel and James Orange to organize in Montgomery during her college years:
interview_snippets.quote: Well, those college days were spent, as I said, SCLC had formed and sent James Bevel and James Orange into Montgomery.

sources.title: Adam Fairclough, To Redeem the Soul of America: The Southern Christian Leadership Conference and Martin Luther King, Jr.
sources.publisher: University of Georgia Press, 1987; WorldCat OCLC 14933060
